Details: Both outputs are comprehensive PySpark unit testing implementations but address completely different business domains. Agent_1 focuses on financial transaction aggregation with 7 test cases covering filtering, joining, and aggregation operations. Agent_2 targets PnP ACH daily processing with 13 test cases covering entity classification, regex matching, and business logic validation. While both demonstrate solid testing practices, they serve entirely different purposes with minimal semantic overlap.

The outputs address fundamentally different business problems. Agent_1 tests transaction aggregation logic (filtering active transactions, date ranges, joining with accounts, computing sums/averages). Agent_2 tests PnP customer classification logic (regex pattern matching for PERSON/BUSINESS entities, merchant details joining, audit logging). Both use PySpark DataFrames and pytest framework, but the core business logic, data schemas, and functional requirements are completely different. Only shared concepts are basic DataFrame operations and test structure.

Both outputs follow similar pytest structural patterns: fixture setup for SparkSession, helper functions for DataFrame creation, individual test functions with descriptive names, and proper test isolation. Agent_1 uses 7 test functions with clear naming (test_happy_path, test_only_active_transactions, etc.). Agent_2 uses 13 test functions following similar naming conventions (test_TC01_happy_path, test_TC02_empty_inputs, etc.). Both include schema definitions, sample data creation, and assertion patterns. However, Agent_2 includes additional complexity with monkeypatch for mocking external functions, while Agent_1 focuses on direct DataFrame operations.

Agent_1 demonstrates strong syntactic correctness with proper PySpark imports, valid DataFrame operations, and correct pytest structure. Minor issues: Line 15-20 schema definitions could benefit from explicit nullable parameters, Line 45-50 date filtering logic assumes string-to-date conversion works correctly without explicit error handling. All function calls are properly structured and variable references are consistent.

Agent_2 shows good syntactic structure but has several areas of concern. Line 25-30 schema definitions are incomplete for some DataFrames, Line 85-90 monkeypatch usage assumes functions exist in __name__ module which may cause import issues, Line 150-155 some test functions have placeholder assertions (assert True) rather than meaningful validations. The mock function implementations are simplified and may not reflect actual business logic complexity.

Average correctness score of 88.5 rounded to 89. Both outputs demonstrate competent PySpark and pytest usage with proper structure and mostly valid syntax. Agent_1 shows slightly better implementation completeness while Agent_2 shows more comprehensive test coverage but with some implementation gaps.

Enhance error handling for date conversions and null value processing. Add explicit schema validation and consider edge cases for malformed data. Implement more granular assertions to validate specific business rules beyond basic aggregation correctness.

Replace placeholder assertions with meaningful validations. Improve mock function implementations to better simulate actual business logic. Add proper error handling for external function dependencies and consider integration test scenarios beyond unit test scope.

Both outputs would benefit from: 1) More comprehensive documentation of business rules being tested, 2) Addition of performance test cases for large datasets, 3) Integration with CI/CD pipeline considerations, 4) Standardized error message validation across test cases.
